name: Dandelion Greens

supplementary_research.finding: Dandelion leaf and root extracts improve lipid profiles (reduced total cholesterol and triglycerides), exert anti-hypertensive effects via antioxidant capacity and lipid peroxidation reduction, and inhibit platelet aggregation through suppression of arachidonic acid metabolism. Chicoric acid inhibits platelet surface receptor exposition. PMID: 35405963.

Dandelion phytochemicals (taraxasterol, luteolin, quercetin) activate autophagy via mTOR/4EBP1 inhibition, induce apoptosis through Bcl-2/caspase pathways, and arrest cell cycle via p21/p53 upregulation. Demonstrates anti-proliferative effects across breast, colorectal, gastric, and lung cancer cell lines. PMID: 41374059.

Dandelion greens are among the highest dietary sources of vitamin K (778 mcg/100g, ~649% DV) and contain significant vitamin A (508 mcg RAE), beta-carotene (5,854 mcg), and lutein+zeaxanthin (13,610 mcg per 100g), supporting vision health, bone density, and anti-inflammatory defense. Generally recognized as safe (GRAS) by FDA.

seasonality: Spring (peak March–May); available year-round from cultivated sources

nutrient_highlights.bioavailability_notes: Fat-soluble; absorption significantly enhanced when consumed with olive oil or other dietary fats. One of the richest food sources of vitamin K among common greens.
Conversion to retinol enhanced by co-consumption with fat. Also contains lutein + zeaxanthin at ~13,610 mcg/100g, supporting macular health.
A potent NF-κB inhibitor and mTOR modulator. Bioavailability enhanced by mild cooking; pairs well with quercetin from onions for additive anti-inflammatory effect.
Fermented by Bifidobacterium and Lactobacillus species in the colon; supports gut microbiome diversity linked to reduced systemic inflammation and healthy aging.
Bioavailable form; supports blood pressure regulation and cardiovascular health. Retained well in sautéed preparations.

synergies.description: Fat-soluble carotenoids (beta-carotene, lutein) and vitamin K in dandelion greens require dietary fat for absorption. Extra-virgin olive oil also contributes oleocanthal (anti-inflammatory) and oleic acid, amplifying the overall anti-inflammatory and cardiovascular-protective effect of the pairing.

Vitamin C in lemon juice (and dandelion itself) enhances non-heme iron absorption from dandelion greens (3.1 mg/100g). The acidity also balances bitterness and may improve polyphenol stability during cooking.

Allicin and diallyl sulfides in garlic complement dandelion's flavonoids (luteolin, quercetin) with additive NF-κB and mTOR suppression. The combination appears across Italian and Middle Eastern longevity cuisines as a foundational bitter-green preparation.

Beans provide plant protein and resistant starch that buffer dandelion's bitterness; dandelion's inulin adds prebiotic fiber to the beans' fiber load, supporting a robust gut microbiome. Classic pairing in Italian cucina povera and Blue Zone-adjacent diets.
